programme: Bachelor of Science (Honours) in Intelligent Robotics
website_title: Adapt and Innovate, Enrol in a Robotics Course in Malaysia | MMU
page_meta_description: Explore robotics in Malaysia with MMU's Bachelor of Science in Intelligent Robotics. Gain hands-on experience in multi-disciplinary programme. See the requirements here.
faculty: Faculty of Artificial Intelligence and Engineering (FAIE)
description: The Bachelor of Science (Honours) Intelligent Robotics is a 3-year programme that strikes an exquisite balance between the fundamentals of engineering and hands-on practical skills. This multidisciplinary programme combines electronics, robotics, artificial intelligence, automation, and computer programming. It adopts a modern learning approach with early exposure to real-world applications. Graduates will be agile knowledge workers in the IR4.0 age and beyond, highly sought after by the industry.
university_subjects: Character Building Program: Character Building and Sustainable Society
Fundamentals of Digital Competence for Programmers
U1 - Penghayatan Etika dan Peradaban Isu Semasa (local students) / Bahasa Melayu Komunikasi 2 (international students)
U2 - Bahasa Kebangsaan A / Foreign Language
entry_requirements: Pass Foundation or Matriculation studies in a related field from a recognised institution with a minimum CGPA of 2.00.
Pass STPM or equivalent with a minimum Grade C (GP 2.00) in any 2 subjects.
Pass A-Level with a minimum Grade D in any two subjects.
Pass UEC with a minimum Grade B in at least five subjects.
Pass STAM with minimum grade of Jayyid.
Recognised Diploma in a related field with a minimum CGPA of 2.00 or equivalent.
Pass DKM, DLKM or DVM with a minimum CGPA of 2.50; candidates below 2.50 must have at least two years of related work experience.
Other relevant and equivalent qualifications recognised by the Malaysian Government.
Possess an APEL.A certificate from MQA for admission into Bachelor programmes.
entry_requirement_note: DKM / DLKM / DVM candidates may be required to undergo a Bridging Programme as an additional requirement.

subject_master.contact_hours: 48 hours (Lecture Hours = 35 and Supervised Tutorial Hours = 13)
subject_master.objective: To provide necessary grounding in calculus, algebra, vectors, complex functions, Fourier analysis and probability to students pursuing engineering and science degrees.
course_contents: Complex number functions and vector algebra
clos: Compute the limits, derivatives, integrals and extrema of single-variable functions.
Perform basic vector operations and calculate the powers and roots of complex numbers.
Construct the power series and the Fourier series representations of functions.
Determine the probability, mean and variance for various distributions in discrete and continuous random variables.
subject_master.full_text:
Subject Code: ARM6113

Title: Technical Calculus

Category: core

Group: technical

Year: 1

Trimester: 1

Intake Cycle Label: March/April 2026

Credit Hours: 3

Contact Hours: 48 hours (Lecture Hours = 35 and Supervised Tutorial Hours = 13)

Pre-Requisite: None shown in structure

Objective:
To provide necessary grounding in calculus, algebra, vectors, complex functions, Fourier analysis and probability to students pursuing engineering and science degrees.

Assessment:
- Tests/Quizzes: 40%
- Final Examination: 60%

Course Contents:
- Calculus of one variable
- Fourier series
- Complex number functions and vector algebra
- Probability

Course Learning Outcomes:
- Compute the limits, derivatives, integrals and extrema of single-variable functions.
- Perform basic vector operations and calculate the powers and roots of complex numbers.
- Construct the power series and the Fourier series representations of functions.
- Determine the probability, mean and variance for various distributions in discrete and continuous random variables.

Source Structure: BSc-IR-March-2026-T2610.pdf

Source Detail: Subject Code all.docx

subject_master.objective: To provide a basic understanding of the laws of circuit theory and the behaviour of circuits during steady-state and transient conditions.
laboratory: CK1: Thevenin’s Theorem and Superposition Theorem
clos: Explain the basic concepts and laws of electric circuits, and different types of signals.
Solve the electrical networks using nodal and mesh analysis techniques.
Compute the impedance, resonance and complex power of sinusoidal circuits.
Analyse the transient response of RL, RC and RLC circuits.
Construct a circuit by using circuit theory experiment board to verify the law of electric circuit and phasor relationship.
subject_master.full_text:
Subject Code: ARL6113

Title: Electrical Circuits

Category: core

Group: technical

Year: 1

Trimester: 1

Intake Cycle Label: March/April 2026

Credit Hours: 3

Contact Hours: 46 hours (lectures, tutorials and laboratory experiments)

Pre-Requisite: None shown in structure

Objective:
To provide a basic understanding of the laws of circuit theory and the behaviour of circuits during steady-state and transient conditions.

Assessment:
- Test/Quiz: 20%
- Assignment: 20%
- Lab: 10%
- Final Examination: 50%

Laboratory:
- CK1: Thevenin’s Theorem and Superposition Theorem
- CK2: AC Circuits

Course Contents:
- Circuit elements and signal waveforms
- Basic laws of electric circuit
- Network analysis
- Sinusoidal steady state analysis
- Transient analysis

Course Learning Outcomes:
- Explain the basic concepts and laws of electric circuits, and different types of signals.
- Solve the electrical networks using nodal and mesh analysis techniques.
- Compute the impedance, resonance and complex power of sinusoidal circuits.
- Analyse the transient response of RL, RC and RLC circuits.
- Construct a circuit by using circuit theory experiment board to verify the law of electric circuit and phasor relationship.

Source Structure: BSc-IR-March-2026-T2610.pdf

Source Detail: Subject Code all.docx

subject_master.objective: To provide a sound understanding of basic electronic devices such as diodes, transistors, and their respective applications.
course_contents: P-N junction characteristics and diode models
Bipolar junction transistor and biasing techniques
Transistor amplifier and small-signal equivalent circuits
clos: Explain the fundamental properties of semiconductor material and characteristics of PN junction as circuit element.
Analyse the diode and bipolar junction transistor (BJT) circuits.
Analyse the transistor amplifier circuits.
Demonstrate the diode and transistor circuits.

subject_master.objective: To introduce students to differential equations with focus on the ordinary differential equations and partial differential equations, as well as the requisite concepts and analysis tools to understand and solve them, including partial differentiation and integral transforms.
clos: Solve differential equations using various methods.
Apply concepts of calculus to solve multivariable problems.
Determine appropriate integral transforms to solve boundary or initial value problem, and difference equations.

subject_master.objective: To introduce students to computing and teach them basic programming skills.
subject_master.laboratory: Supervised tutorials done in many computer labs, including Multimedia Design Lab, Multimedia Computing Lab, etc.
clos: Demonstrate the use of arrays, pointers, characters and strings, formatted input/output, and other data structures in computer programs.
Construct self-defined functions in computer programs.
Apply the techniques used to process text files in computer programs.
Design complete and properly structured programs.

subject_master.objective: To provide a basic understanding of the basics of logic circuits and their applications in digital system.
laboratory: DL1: Logic Gates And Their Applications
course_contents: Algebraic methods for analysis and synthesis of logic circuits
clos: Distinguish the analog and digital systems, apply positional notations, number systems and computer codes in digital systems.
Use Boolean algebra, the Karnaugh maps and Quine-McCluskey tabular methods to simplify and design logic circuits.
Apply the concepts of sequential logic and memory devices in digital systems.
Design modular combinational circuits using encoders, decoders, multiplexers and demultiplexers.

Intake Cycle Label: October/November 2026

subject_master.objective: To acquire technical knowledge in the field of actuators and sensors, and to design programmable logic controllers used in various industrial applications.
course_contents: Basic principle of actuators and sensors
clos: State the general operating principles of actuator, sensor and gear, and their technical properties in a basic drive system.

Demonstrate the usage of sensors in industrial applications.
Construct programmable logic controllers with sensor and actuator using practical software tools.

Develop assembly language programs for 8051 based embedded systems.

Subject Code: ARC6133

Title: Advanced Programming

Group: technical

Trimester: 2

To provide undergraduates with a basic comprehension of the C++ programming language and the concepts of Object Oriented Programming (OOP).

- Final exam: 50%

- Object oriented and C++ fundamentals

- Dynamic memory allocation
- Inheritance and polymorphism
- Basic OO design using UML
- Advanced C++ concepts and applications

Course Learning Outcomes:

- Construct relationship between classes and objects using C++ code and UML class diagram.
- Evaluate the usage of function templates and class templates in a program.

clos: Use various methods in linear algebra to solve systems of algebraic equations.
Apply numerical methods to solve mathematical problems including interpolation, differentiation, integration, root finding, ordinary differential equations and systems of linear equations.
Determine the optimal solutions to the optimization problems using appropriate methods.

Analyse transient response, error and stability of control systems using time domain and root locus methods.
Analyse properties of control systems using frequency domain methods.
Design compensators for control systems using strategic thinking.

subject_master.objective: To provides students with rigorous mathematical skills on modelling and control of robotic arm and similar manipulator.
subject_master.laboratory: Control and automation lab, Robotic arms.
clos: Compute the pose of a robotic arm given the configuration, links and angle of each joint.
Deduce the joint angle of each robotic arm link given the required pose via inverse kinematic.
Generate the trajectory for the end-effector of a robotic arm via joint-space or Cartesian space schemes using anticipatory thinking.
Construct linear trajectory-following control scheme for robotic arm.
subject_master.full_text:
Subject Code: ARR6143

Title: Robotics – Modelling and Control

Aliases: Robotics - Modelling and Control

Category: core

Group: technical

Year: 3

Trimester: 1

Intake Cycle Label: March/April 2028

Credit Hours: 3

Contact Hours: 44 hours (lectures, tutorials and laboratory experiments)

To provides students with rigorous mathematical skills on modelling and control of robotic arm and similar manipulator.

- Test: 20%
- Assignment: 20%
- Lab Test: 20%
- Final exam: 40%

Laboratory:
Control and automation lab, Robotic arms.

Course Contents:
- Representing position and orientation
- Robot modeling
- Forward and inverse kinematics
- Trajectory generation
- Velocity relationships
- Dynamic and control

Course Learning Outcomes:
- Compute the pose of a robotic arm given the configuration, links and angle of each joint.
- Deduce the joint angle of each robotic arm link given the required pose via inverse kinematic.
- Generate the trajectory for the end-effector of a robotic arm via joint-space or Cartesian space schemes using anticipatory thinking.
- Construct linear trajectory-following control scheme for robotic arm.

Year: 3

Trimester: 2

Intake Cycle Label: July/August 2028

Credit Hours: 3

Contact Hours: 41 hours

Pre-Requisite: None

Objective:
The course aims to introduce the knowledge and skills of the digital competence and its framework elements. It also exposes learners to the emergence of digital competence as a new approach and skills needed to manage daily life tasks and industry.

Assessment:
- Assignment 1: 30%
- Assignment 2: 30%
- Project: 40%

Course Contents:
- Digital competence and its framework
- Information and data literacy
- Communication and collaboration
- Digital content creation
- Safety in digital world
- Problem solving in digital world
- Distributed version control and Git workflows

Course Learning Outcomes:
- Describe measures for digital safety and solutions for common digital problems in a modern workplace environment.
- Demonstrate ability to work with digital information collaboratively through collaboration and communication tools.
- Build a program collaboratively with digital content.

Source Structure: BSc-IR-March-2026-T2610.pdf

Source Detail: LDCW6123 — Fundamentals of Digital Competence for Programmers.pdf
